title: The Twenty-Fifth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Sermon: "The Twenty-Fifth Sunday after Pentecost" | Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver
Preacher: Broderick Greer | Date: November 14, 2021

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on the Twenty-Fifth Sunday after Pentecost, placing the service in Ordinary Time following Pentecost Sunday (the post-Pentecost period before Advent begins). According to the Revised Common Lectionary for this Sunday, the readings likely included Zephaniah 1:7, 12-18 (judgment day), 1 Thessalonians 5:1-11 (the day of the Lord), Matthew 25:14-30 (the parable of the talents), and Psalm 9. The liturgical calendar would have been approaching the First Sunday of Advent (November 28, 2021), marking the beginning of the new church year.

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was navigating pandemic recovery and theological tensions over sexuality and inclusion that had marked the 2018 General Convention. No major diocesan elections or denominational controversies appear to have peaked specifically in mid-November 2021, though the Church was in the ongoing period of implementation following the 79th General Convention held in Austin, Texas in July 2018. The Diocese of Colorado was operating under Bishop Robert J. O'Neill's leadership (he served 2001-2020, though exact transition timing requires verification; this may reflect a period of diocesan transition or new leadership consolidation).

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Colorado was in the midst of fall weather patterns with temperatures dropping into the 40s-50s Fahrenheit range in mid-November. Denver was preparing for the holiday season, with light displays and community events beginning around Thanksgiving week. No major crisis events appear to have dominated Denver headlines in the immediate week before November 14, 2021, though the city remained focused on pandemic recovery and downtown revitalization efforts in the Capitol Hill area.

NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Kyle Rittenhouse trial verdict was delivered on November 10, 2021 (acquittal on all charges), dominating national conversation about self-defense, gun rights, and civil unrest. Inflation remained a top economic concern, with the U.S. Consumer Price Index hitting 6.2 percent year-over-year, the highest in 31 years. The Biden administration was focused on infrastructure bill implementation (signed in November) and continued COVID-19 vaccination efforts as cases declined but remained elevated. The Supreme Court was hearing arguments in Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization, signaling the eventual overturning of Roe v. Wade (decision came in June 2022).

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The crisis at the Belarus-Poland border was ongoing, with thousands of migrants (primarily from Middle Eastern countries) attempting to cross into the European Union in harsh November weather. The situation was creating significant diplomatic tensions between Belarus and EU nations. Australia and other Western nations were declaring diplomatic boycotts of the 2022 Beijing Winter Olympics over concerns about human rights in Xinjiang.

CULTURAL MOMENT

America was in a cautiously optimistic but deeply polarized moment following 18 months of pandemic disruption. There was growing cultural division exemplified by the Rittenhouse verdict reaction, ongoing debates about vaccine mandates, and preparations for what would become contentious midterm elections in 2022. Americans were returning to in-person gatherings, and churches like Saint John's were navigating full reopening after COVID closures. The approaching holiday season carried an undertone of relief mixed with lingering anxiety about inflation, supply chain disruptions, and social cohesion. Sports fans were focused on college and professional football (NFL mid-season, NCAA college football playoff positioning), and the Denver Broncos were in a disappointing season at 5-4 record.
